evidence.quote_or_summary: It remains open from December to February and then into spring.
For a magical experience, trek to Brahmatal in early spring (March).
For a quiet experience, aim for dates after Dusshera/Diwali vacation and before winter sets in.

values: Monsoon months (July-August implied)
Mid-April onwards (snow melted)
Summer (not a great season)

evidence.quote_or_summary: Although you can access Brahmatal in summers, it’s not a great season to experience the trek. During monsoons, the trek becomes inaccessible due to the rains.
By mid-April, most of the snow on the trail and around it has melted.

values: Winter: snow-covered landscape, Narnia-like vibe from snow-laden oak and rhododendron trees, frozen Bekaltal lake, snow camping at Tilandi with sunrises/sunsets

Autumn: clear post-monsoon mountain views, Trishul range over Ali Bugyal, views of Mt Chaukhamba, Mt Neelkanth, Mt Hathi Ghoda from Jhandi top, vibrant crimson sunsets and golden sunrises at Tilandi, empty trails
Spring: rhododendron (Buransh) blooms in dark pink and crimson, receding snow, rhodo canopy between Bekaltal and Tilandi, blooms near Khorurai

evidence.quote_or_summary: In winters, oak and rhododendron trees stoop under the weight of the snow lending a Narnia-like vibe to the trek.
the sight of the frozen Bekaltal lake is mesmerising.
camping on the snow at Tilandi with spectacular sunrises and sunsets.
the mountain views. They’re the highlight in autumn, at their clearest post-monsoon when the air is free from haze.
brilliant mountain views of Mt Chaukhamba, Mt Neelkanth, and Mt Hathi Ghoda open up under the blue autumn sky.
The sun splashes vibrant crimson hues across the sky. And the next morning, wake up early to watch the golden sunrise on the other side.
Its slopes are lined with rhododendrons (Buransh). And in spring, the slopes look fiery with rhodos blooming dark pink and crimson.
just as you step out of Bekaltal, you enter a rhodo forest and the blooming flowers form a canopy over your head as you trek.

values: From end of December at base camp; entire landscape white in peak winter (January-February); upper reaches still under snow in spring; snow recedes to higher altitudes (around Brahmatal lake at 12,250 ft) as spring progresses; mostly melted by mid-April

evidence.quote_or_summary: You will find snow from the base camp towards the end of December. The entire landscape and valley will be in white
The upper reaches of Brahmatal are still under snow
you will find snow in sections upwards of Tilandi. As the season goes by, snow recedes to the higher altitude, around the Brahmatal lake which lies at 12,250 ft.
By mid-April, most of the snow on the trail and around it has melted.

values: Monsoon makes the trek inaccessible due to rains

evidence.quote_or_summary: During monsoons, the trek becomes inaccessible due to the rains.

values: Winter: Day 8-10°C, Night 0 to -8°C; 5 warm layers required
Autumn: Day 10-15°C, Night 0 to -5°C; 4 warm layers required
Spring: Day 15-18°C, Night 0 to 5°C; 3 warm layers required

evidence.quote_or_summary: Day time: Between 8 °C and 10 °C | Night time: Will drop to temperatures between 0 °C and -8 °C. No. of warm layers required: 5 warm layers
Day time: Between 10 °C and 15 °C | Night time: Will drop to temperatures between 0 °C and -5 °C. No. of warm layers required: 4 warm layers
Day time: Between 15 °C and 18 °C | Night time: Will drop to temperatures between 0 °C and 5 °C. No. of warm layers required: 3 warm layers

values: Autumn offers clearest post-monsoon mountain views; Trishul range visible from ridge parallel to Ali Bugyal; Mt Chaukhamba, Mt Neelkanth, Mt Hathi Ghoda visible from Jhandi top; vibrant crimson sunsets and golden sunrises at Tilandi ridge

evidence.quote_or_summary: the mountain views. They’re the highlight in autumn, at their clearest post-monsoon when the air is free from haze.
From here you can see the grand Trishul range rise over Asia’s largest meadow.
brilliant mountain views of Mt Chaukhamba, Mt Neelkanth, and Mt Hathi Ghoda open up under the blue autumn sky.
The sun splashes vibrant crimson hues across the sky. And the next morning, wake up early to watch the golden sunrise on the other side.

values: Do not step on frozen Bekaltal lake — ice may not support weight depending on month
Brahmatal lake/top may not be accessible in peak winter due to snow
Winter trek is a notch more difficult due to snow
Summer is not a great season despite access; monsoon makes trek inaccessible

evidence.quote_or_summary: But be careful about stepping on this frozen lake. Depending on the month, the ice may or may not be hard enough to support your weight.
Brahmatal lake or top may not be accessible in the peak winters due to snow.
Although it makes the trek a notch more difficult.
Although you can access Brahmatal in summers, it’s not a great season to experience the trek. During monsoons, the trek becomes inaccessible due to the rains.

values: Snow lovers and winter trekkers (Dec-Feb)
Trekkers seeking solitude (autumn, after Dusshera/Diwali and before winter)
Photographers of vibrant sunrises/sunsets (autumn at Tilandi)
Rhododendron bloom seekers (spring, especially early March for snow + flowers)
Trekkers wanting clear Himalayan views (autumn)

evidence.quote_or_summary: winter snow is heavenly on the Brahmatal trek.
autumn is also a great season if you like empty trails.
sunrises and sunsets. They are the most vibrant during autumn.
Brahmatal too is a charming trek to do in spring. Its slopes are lined with rhododendrons (Buransh).
For a magical experience, trek to Brahmatal in early spring (March). So that you get to trek on snow even as you see the flowers in bloom.
